cell.rationale: A clear single clause governs the act — the retrieved Law states that a player is sent off for denying the opposing team a goal or an obvious goal-scoring opportunity by committing a deliberate handball offence (ifab-law12-dogso-handball-p118).
The act is admitted, so the deliberateness residual is resolved.

The decisive truth was available at the moment — the retrieved facts state that the referee saw the handball in real time, applied the Law correctly by sending off Suárez and awarding a penalty to Ghana (suarez-hist-correctly-adjudicated).

Luis Suárez is reported to have called the handball 'the save of the tournament' and said the Hand of God now belonged to him, framing the handball as a worthwhile sacrifice for the team (suarez-framing-suarez). In contrast, Asamoah Gyan described the moment as a cruel injustice that denied Ghana a deserved place in the semi-final, a wound that the missed penalty made permanent (suarez-framing-gyan). These two named people describe one agreed event in opposing terms.

ifab-law12-dogso-handball-p118.extracted_text: Where a player denies the opposing team a goal or an obvious goal-scoring opportunity by committing a deliberate handball offence the player is sent off whatever the position of the offence.

ifab-law12-handball-offence-p110.extracted_text: It is an offence if a player deliberately touches the ball with their hand/arm, or scores in the opponents' goal immediately after the ball has touched their hand/arm, even if accidental. A goal scored in this way does not stand.

suarez-framing-gyan.attribution: Asamoah Gyan, post-match and subsequent interviews (2010-07-03)
suarez-framing-gyan.extracted_text: Asamoah Gyan (2010-07-03) — CONDEMNS: Gyan is reported to have described the moment as a cruel injustice that denied Ghana a deserved place in the semi-final, a wound that the missed penalty made permanent.

suarez-framing-suarez.extracted_text: Luis Suárez (2010-07-03) — JUSTIFIES: Suárez is reported to have called it the "save of the tournament" and said the Hand of God now belonged to him, framing the handball as a worthwhile sacrifice for the team.

suarez-hist-correctly-adjudicated.attribution: Curated historical record — Suárez's handball on the line
suarez-hist-correctly-adjudicated.extracted_text: The referee saw the handball in real time and applied the Law exactly: Suárez was sent off and a penalty was awarded to Ghana. The officiating decision itself was correct and uncontested.

suarez-hist-on-the-line.attribution: Curated historical record — Suárez's handball on the line
suarez-hist-on-the-line.extracted_text: In the final minute of extra time, Luis Suárez deliberately handled a goal-bound ball on the goal line to stop a certain Ghana goal. The act was clear and admitted.

output.rationale: The retrieved Law states that a player is sent off for denying the opposing team a goal or an obvious goal-scoring opportunity by committing a deliberate handball offence (ifab-law12-dogso-handball-p118). The Law also specifies that a player commits a handball offence if they deliberately touch the ball with their hand/arm, or score in the opponents' goal immediately after the ball has touched their hand/arm, and a goal scored in this way does not stand (ifab-law12-handball-offence-p110). Therefore, there is no ambiguity as both clauses point to the same outcome for the same facts.
No evidence was retrieved for this lens on this incident.

The retrieved facts state that the referee saw the handball in real time, applied the Law correctly by sending off Suárez and awarding a penalty to Ghana (suarez-hist-correctly-adjudicated). However, it also mentions that Suárez clearly and admittedly handled the ball on the goal line to prevent a certain Ghana goal (suarez-hist-on-the-line). This indicates that while the officials at the moment could access the decisive truth and made a correct decision, the incident itself involved a contentious act of handball that could have been disputed due to its clear nature and Suárez's admission.

Luis Suárez is reported to have called the handball 'the save of the tournament' and said the Hand of God now belonged to him, framing the handball as a worthwhile sacrifice for the team (suarez-framing-suarez). In contrast, Asamoah Gyan described the moment as a cruel injustice that denied Ghana a deserved place in the semi-final, a wound that the missed penalty made permanent (suarez-framing-gyan). These two named people describe one agreed event in opposing terms.

settled_fact.statement: In the last minute against Ghana in the 2010 World Cup, Luis Suárez deliberately handled a goal-bound ball on the line. He was sent off and a penalty was awarded — the call was correct. Ghana missed the penalty and Uruguay won the shootout. The handball, and the red card for it, are not in dispute.

split.headline: It stays contested over how named sources frame the same fact — not over the dimensions ruled out.
